Since all reads have a poly(A) tail in Nanopore direct RNA sequencing, we could standardize the reads such that the mean and std of poly(A) part is the same for all reads. The standardization is given by(1)standardized_signal=raw_signal−μpolyAσpolyA∗σstandPolyA+μstandPolyA\begin{document}$$\displaystyle standardized\_signal = \frac{raw\_signal - \mu_{polyA} }{\sigma_{polyA}}* \sigma_{stand\,PolyA}+ \mu_{stand\,PolyA}$$\end{document}

where μstandPolyA=108.9\begin{document}$\mu_{standPolyA}= 108.9$\end{document} and σstandPolyA=1.67\begin{document}$\sigma_{standPolyA}= 1.67$\end{document} (RNA002). Note that yi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y_{i}}$\end{document} refers to the standardized signal throughout this document.

sec.title: Hierarchical hidden Markov model
sec.p: This section describes the hierarchical hidden Markov model (HHMM) to segment the raw current signal of a read. The input of HHMM is yi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y_i}$\end{document} and the output is zi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{z_i}$\end{document}. For notation simplicity, we use y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document} and z\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{z}$\end{document} to represent yi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y_i}$\end{document} and zi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{z_i}$\end{document} in this section, respectively.

sec.title: Model assumptions
sec.p: Here we have several assumptions regarding the raw signal. We first assume there is a sharp change of the baseline levels of the current signal between two consecutive 5-mers. This means there is a large change of current signal within a short period of time. If we fit a line to the corresponding signals, we expect to see a large slope. The second assumption is that the 5-mer resides in the Nanopore, oscillates forward and backward during a 5-mer event, while staying in the pore most of the time. Based on these two assumptions, we can divide the raw current signal into two states (hidden states of the outer HMM): the base states for 5-mer event and the transition state for the transition between two consecutive 5-mer events. We assume that the current signal always starts with a base state, then switches to a transition state, after that switches back to a base state or transition state alternately, and in the end, the signal should end in a base state. For the base state, we use an inner HMM to model the oscillations, which has four hidden states: ‘curr’, ‘prev’, ‘next’, and ‘noise’. For the transition state, we use a linear regression model to model the abrupt change of the current signal.

sec.title: Outer HMM
sec.p: We define the following notations for the HHMM. As illustrated in Appendix 1—figure 2, we assume there are N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} time points of the current signal, that is y=(y1,y2,⋯,yi,⋯,yN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}= (y_{1}, y_{2}, \cdots, y_{i}, \cdots, y_{N})$\end{document}. We use g=(g1,g2,⋯,gi,⋯,gN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}= (g_{1}, g_{2}, \cdots, g_{i}, \cdots , g_{N})$\end{document}, gi∈{‘‘B",‘‘T"}\begin{document}$g_{i}\, \in \left\{``B", ``T"\right\}$\end{document} to indicate the hidden states of the outer HMM, where B\begin{document}$B$\end{document} and T\begin{document}$T$\end{document} stand for ‘base’ and ‘transition’, respectively. Similarly, we use h=(h1,h2,⋯,hi,⋯,hN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{h}= (h_{1}, h_{2}, \cdots, h_{i}, \cdots, h_{N})$\end{document}, hi∈{‘‘curr",‘‘prev",‘‘next",‘‘noise"}\begin{document}$h_{i}\in \{``curr", ``prev", ``next", ``noise"\}$\end{document}, respectively. We then integrate g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document} and h\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{h}$\end{document} into a single vector z=(z1,z2,⋯,zi,⋯,zN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{z}=(z_{1}, z_{2}, \cdots, z_{i}, \cdots, z_{N})$\end{document}, where zi=g~i⋅h~i\begin{document}$z_{i}=\tilde{g}_{i} \cdot \tilde{h}_{i}$\end{document}. Here g~i\begin{document}$\tilde{g}_{i}$\end{document} (‘‘B"→1,‘‘T"→0)\begin{document}$(``B" \rightarrow 1, ``T" \rightarrow 0)$\end{document} and h~i\begin{document}$\tilde{h}_{i}$\end{document} (‘curr’→1, ‘prev’→2, ‘next’→3, ‘noise’→4) are the numerical mappings of gi\begin{document}$g_{i}$\end{document} and hi\begin{document}$h_{i}$\end{document}. Based on g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document}, we can derive that y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document} is divided into 2K+1\begin{document}$2K+1$\end{document} blocks, which is defined by c=(c1,c2,⋯,ci,⋯,c2K+1)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{c}= (c_{1}, c_{2}, \cdots, c_{i}, \cdots, c_{2K+1})$\end{document}, ci∈{‘‘B",‘‘T"}\begin{document}$c_{i}\in \{``B", ``T"\}$\end{document}. Note that ci=‘‘B"∀i=1,3,⋯,2K+1\begin{document}$c_{i}=``B"\: \forall i=1,3,\cdots,2K+1$\end{document} and ci=‘‘T"∀i=2,4,⋯,2K\begin{document}$c_{i}=``T" \: \forall i=2,4,\cdots,2K$\end{document}. As a result, we could also represent y=(y(1),y(2),⋯,y(i),⋯,y(2K+1))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}=(\boldsymbol{y}^{(1)}, \boldsymbol{y}^{(2)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(i)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(2K+1)})$\end{document}, where y(i)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(i)}$\end{document} represent the ith block. The base blocks are given by (y(b1),y(b2),⋯,y(bi),⋯,y(bK+1))\begin{document}$(\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_1)}, \boldsymbol{y}^{(b_2)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(b_{K+1})})$\end{document}, where bi=2i−1\begin{document}$b_{i}=2i-1$\end{document} and i∈{1,2,⋯,K+1}\begin{document}$i\in \{1, 2, \cdots, K+1\}$\end{document}. The transition blocks are given by (y(t1),y(t2),⋯,y(ti),⋯,y(tK))\begin{document}$(\boldsymbol{y}^{(t_1)}, \boldsymbol{y}^{(t_2)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(t_{K})})$\end{document}, where ti=2i\begin{document}$t_{i}=2i$\end{document} and i∈{1,2,⋯,K}\begin{document}$i\in \{1, 2, \cdots, K\}$\end{document}. Similarly, we have g=(g(1),g(2),⋯,g(i),⋯,g(2K+1))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}=(\boldsymbol{g}^{(1)}, \boldsymbol{g}^{(2)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{g}^{(i)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{g}^{(2K+1)})$\end{document} and h=(h(1),h(2),⋯,h(i),⋯,h(2K+1))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{h}=(\boldsymbol{h}^{(1)}, \boldsymbol{h}^{(2)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{h}^{(i)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{h}^{(2K+1)})$\end{document}.

caption.title: Example of HHMM where gi∈{‘‘B",‘‘T"}\begin{document}$g_{i}\,\in \left\{``B",\,``T"\right\} $\end{document}, hi∈{‘‘curr",‘‘prev",‘‘next",‘‘noise"}\begin{document}$h_{i}\,\in \,\{``curr",\,``prev",\,``next",\,``noise"\} $\end{document} and ci∈{‘‘B",‘‘T"}\begin{document}$c_{i}\,\in \left\{``B",\,``T"\right\} $\end{document}.
caption.p: In this figure, ‘c’, ‘p’, ‘n’, and ‘o’ represent ‘curr’, ‘prev’, ‘next’, and ‘noise’ respectively in h\begin{document}$\boldsymbol h$\end{document}. |y|=|g|=|h|and|c|=11\begin{document}$\left|y\right| =\left|g\right| =\left|h\right| {\rm and}\left|c\right| =11$\end{document}.

sec.p: We use Touter=\begin{document}$T^{outer}=$\end{document}{TBBouter,TBTouter,TTBouter,TTTouter}\begin{document}$\{ T^{outer}_{BB}, T^{outer}_{BT}, T^{outer}_{TB}, T^{outer}_{TT}\}$\end{document} to denote the transition probabilities between the base state and the transition state in the outer HMM. We use Tinner=\begin{document}$T^{inner}=$\end{document} {Thihjinner∀hi,hj∈{‘‘curr",‘‘prev",‘‘next",‘‘noise"}}\begin{document}$\{T^{inner}_{{h_i}{h_j}} \forall h_{i}, h_{j}\in \{``curr", ``prev", ``next",``noise"\} \}$\end{document} to denote the transition probabilities between four hidden states of the inner HMM. For base blocks, we have inner HMM parameters ϕ=(ϕ(b1),ϕ(b2),⋯,ϕ(bK+1))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\phi}= (\phi^{(b_1)}, \phi^{(b_2)}, \cdots,\phi^{(b_{K+1})})$\end{document} and σ=(σ(b1),σ(b2),⋯,σ(bK+1))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\sigma}= (\sigma^{(b_1)}, \sigma^{(b_2)}, \cdots,\sigma^{(b_{K+1})})$\end{document}. For transition blocks, we have linear coefficients β0=(β0(t1),β0(t2),⋯,β0(tK))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\beta}_{0}= (\beta_{0}^{(t_1)}, \beta_{0}^{(t_2)}, \cdots,\beta_{0}^{(t_K)})$\end{document}, β1=(β1(t1),β1(t2),⋯,β1(tK))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\beta}_{1}= (\beta_{1}^{(t_1)}, \beta_{1}^{(t_2)}, \cdots,\beta_{1}^{(t_K)})$\end{document} and noise std σϵ=(σϵ(t1),σϵ(t2),⋯,σϵ(tK))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\sigma_{\epsilon}}= (\sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_1)}, \sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_2)}, \cdots,\sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_K)})$\end{document}.

The joint likelihood of the HHMM is given by(2)p(y,g)=p(y∣g)p(g)=p(y∣g)πg1outer∏i=2NTgi−1giouter=∏i=1Np(yi∣gi)πg1outer∏i=2NTgi−1giouter,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll} p(\boldsymbol {y, g})& = p(\boldsymbol y \mid \boldsymbol g)\, p( \boldsymbol g) \\[6pt] \quad &= p(\boldsymbol y \mid \boldsymbol g)\, \pi_{g_1}^{outer} \prod_{i=2}^{N} T_{g_{i-1} g_i}^{outer} \\[6pt] \quad &= \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(y_i \mid g_i)\, \pi_{g_1}^{outer} \prod_{i=2}^{N} T_{g_{i-1} g_i}^{outer} \end{array},$$\end{document}

where πg1outer=1\begin{document}$\pi^{outer}_{g_1}=1$\end{document} is the initial probability of hidden state of the y1\begin{document}$y_{1}$\end{document} (always ‘B’ state). However, it is not possible to directly derive p(yi,hi|gi)\begin{document}$p(y_{i}, h_{i}| g_{i})$\end{document}, since yi\begin{document}$y_{i}$\end{document} are not independent given gi\begin{document}$g_{i}$\end{document}. Here we use different models for each block ck\begin{document}$c_{k}$\end{document} to calculate the joint probability p(y(k)|g(k))\begin{document}$p(\boldsymbol{y}^{(k)}| \boldsymbol{g}^{(k)})$\end{document} to approximate ∏i=1Np(yi|gi)\begin{document}$\prod_{i=1}^{N}p(y_{i}| g_{i})$\end{document}. Therefore, we have(3)∏i=1Np(yi∣gi)=∏j=02K+1p(y(j)∣cj)=∏j=0Kp(y(2j+1)∣c2j+1=‘‘B")∏j=1Kp(y(2j)∣c2j=‘‘T")\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}\prod_{i=1}^{N} p(y_i \mid g_i) = \prod_{j=0}^{2K+1} p\bigl(y^{(j)} \mid c_j\bigr) \\ = \prod_{j=0}^{K} p\bigl(y^{(2j+1)} \mid c_{2j+1} = {``B"}\bigr) \prod_{j=1}^{K} p\bigl(y^{(2j)} \mid c_{2j} = {``T"}\bigr)\end{array}$$\end{document}

The first part is the marginal likelihood for base blocks, while the second part is the marginal likelihood for transition blocks. The specific likelihood formulas are given in the following sections.

For the outer HMM, the transition probabilities Touter\begin{document}$T^{outer}$\end{document} are pre-specified and the emission probabilities are obtained from the inner HMM (‘Inner HMM for base state’) and linear regression model (‘Linear model for transition state’). The initial probability for the first hidden state πg1outer\begin{document}$\pi^{outer}_{g_1}$\end{document} is set to 1, since the first state should always be “B”.

sec.title: Inner HMM for base state
sec.p: This section provides the derivations for calculating the likelihood of the ith base block bi\begin{document}$b_{i}$\end{document}, where i∈{1,2,⋯,K+1}\begin{document}$i\in \{1, 2, \cdots, K+1\}$\end{document}. We assume the data y(bi)=(y1(bi),y2(bi),⋯,yNbi(bi))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}=(y^{(b_i)}_{1}, y^{(b_i)}_{2}, \cdots, y^{(b_i)}_{N_{b_i}})$\end{document} is generated by an HMM with four states: ‘curr’, ‘prev’, ‘next’, and ‘noise’. The descriptions of the states are given by Appendix 1—table 2, where parameters for the noise state are given and parameters for other states need to be estimated from the data.

caption.title: Hidden states of inner HMM for the bi\begin{document}$b_i$\end{document} th block.

sec.p: The likelihood for the kth block is given by(4)p(y(bi)∣cbi=``B``)=∑h(bi)p(y(bi),h(bi)∣cbi=``B``)=∑h(bi)p(y(bi)∣ϕ,σ,h(bi),cbi=``B``)p(h(bi)∣cbi=``B``)=∑h(bi)[∏j=1Nbip(yj(bi)∣hj(bi),ϕ,σ)πh1(bi)inner∏j=2NbiThj−1(bi)hj(bi)inner]=∑h(bi)[∏j=1Nbi{N(yj(bi)∣ϕ(bi),(σ(bi))2)I(hj(bi)=``curr``)N(yj(bi)∣ϕ(bi−1),(σ(bi−1))2)I(hj(bi)=``prev``)N(yj(bi)∣ϕ(bi+1),(σ(bi+1))2)I(hj(bi)=``next``)Unif(yj(bi)∣lb,ub)I(hj(bi)=``noise``)}πh1(bi)inner∏j=2NbiThj−1(bi)hj(bi)inner]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll} p\!\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)} \mid c_{b_i} = \text{``B''}\right) = \sum_{\boldsymbol{h}^{(b_i)}} p\!\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}, \boldsymbol{h}^{(b_i)} \mid c_{b_i} = \text{``B''}\right) \\[6pt] = \sum_{\boldsymbol{h}^{(b_i)}} p\!\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)} \mid \boldsymbol{\phi}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}, \boldsymbol{h}^{(b_i)}, c_{b_i} = \text{``B''}\right) \, p\!\left(\boldsymbol{h}^{(b_i)} \mid c_{b_i} = \text{``B''}\right) \\[6pt] = \sum_{\boldsymbol{h}^{(b_i)}} \left[ \prod_{j=1}^{N_{b_i}} p\!\left(y^{(b_i)}_j \mid h^{(b_i)}_j, \boldsymbol{\phi}, \boldsymbol{\sigma}\right) \;\pi^{\text{inner}}_{h^{(b_i)}_1} \prod_{j=2}^{N_{b_i}} T^{\text{inner}}_{h^{(b_i)}_{j-1} h^{(b_i)}_j} \right] \\[6pt] = \sum_{\boldsymbol{h}^{(b_i)}} \Bigl[\prod_{j=1}^{N_{b_i}} \left\{ N\!\left(y^{(b_i)}_j \mid \phi^{(b_i)}, (\sigma^{(b_i)})^2\right)^{{I}(h^{(b_i)}_j=\text{``curr''})} N\!\left(y^{(b_i)}_j \mid \phi^{(b_i-1)}, (\sigma^{(b_i-1)})^2\right)^{{I}(h^{(b_i)}_j=\text{``prev''})} \right. \\[4pt] \qquad\left. N\!\left(y^{(b_i)}_j \mid \phi^{(b_i+1)}, (\sigma^{(b_i+1)})^2\right)^{{I}(h^{(b_i)}_j=\text{``next''})} \text{Unif}\!\left(y^{(b_i)}_j \mid lb, ub\right)^{{I}(h^{(b_i)}_j=\text{``noise''})} \right\} \\[6pt] \qquad\qquad \pi^{\text{inner}}_{h^{(b_i)}_1} \prod_{j=2}^{N_{b_i}} T^{\text{inner}}_{h^{(b_i)}_{j-1} h^{(b_i)}_j} \Bigr] \end{array}$$\end{document}

where πh1(bi)inner=0.25\begin{document}$\pi^{inner}_{h^{(b_i)}_1}=0.25$\end{document} is the probability of the first hidden state and I(⋅)\begin{document}$I(\cdot)$\end{document} is the indicator function

For the inner HMM, the transition probabilities Tinner\begin{document}$T^{inner}$\end{document} are pre-specified and the initial probability for the first hidden state πh1(bi)inner=0.25\begin{document}$\pi^{inner}_{h^{(b_i)}_1}=0.25$\end{document} is set to 0.25. Other parameters (see Appendix 1—table 2) need to be estimated.

By comparing the emission probabilities of the outer HMM (Equation 3) and the marginal likelihood of the inner HMM (Equation 4), we have(5)p(y(bi)=cbi=‘‘B")=∏j=1|y(bi)|p(yj(bi)|gj(bi))\begin{document}$$\displaystyle p (\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}= c_{b_i}=``B") = \prod_{j=1}^{|\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}|}p({y}^{(b_i)}_{j}|{g}^{(b_i)}_{j})$$\end{document}

To approximate p(yj(bi)|gj(bi))\begin{document}$p({y}^{(b_i)}_{j}|{g}^{(b_i)}_{j})$\end{document}, we assume all values of p(yj(bi)|gj(bi))\begin{document}$p({y}^{(b_i)}_{j}|{g}^{(b_i)}_{j})$\end{document} are the same for any given j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}, we have(6)p(y(bi)|cbi=‘‘B")=∏j=1|y(bi)|p(yj(bi)|gj(bi))=[p^(yj(bi)|gj(bi))]|y(bi)|\begin{document}$$\displaystyle p (\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}\: | \: c_{b_i}=``B") = \prod_{j=1}^{|\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}|}p({y}^{(b_i)}_{j}|{g}^{(b_i)}_{j}) = [\hat{p}({y}^{(b_i)}_{j}|{g}^{(b_i)}_{j})]^{|\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}|}$$\end{document}

The approximated emission probability of each data point in the ith base block is given by(7)p^(yj(bi)∣gj(bi))=p^(y(bi)∣cbi=‘‘B")∣y(bi)∣,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \hat{p} (y^{(b_{i})}_{j}\mid g^{(b_{i})}_{j} )=\sqrt[\mid y^{(b_{i})}\mid ]{\hat{p} (y^{(b_{i})}\mid c_{b_{i}}=``B")},$$\end{document}
where p^(y(bi)|cbi=‘‘B")\begin{document}$\hat{p}\Big(\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}\: | \: c_{b_i}=``B" \Big)$\end{document} can be obtained by our inference algorithm.

Note that the hidden states of the inner HMM h\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{h}$\end{document} can be provided as a by-product of our inference algorithm. We will use it to derive the final composite hidden state z\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{z}$\end{document} of the HHMM (‘Parameter inference’), which is utilized by downstream alignment tasks (‘Post-processing of alignment path’).

sec.title: Linear model for transition state
sec.p: This section provides the derivations for calculating the likelihood of the ith transition block ti\begin{document}$t_{i}$\end{document}, where i∈{1,2,⋯,K}\begin{document}$i\in \{1, 2, \cdots, K\}$\end{document}. We denote the input data by y(ti)=(y1(ti),y2(ti),⋯,yNti(ti))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)}=(y^{(t_i)}_{1}, y^{(t_i)}_{2}, \cdots, y^{(t_i)}_{N_{t_i}})$\end{document} and its corresponding index by x(ti)=(1,2,⋯,Nti)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{x}^{(t_i)}=(1, 2, \cdots, N_{t_i})$\end{document}. We assume a linear model for data points in the transition state, given by(8)y(ti)=β1(ti)x(ti)+β0(ti)+ϵ,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)}= \beta_{1}^{(t_i)}\boldsymbol{x}^{(t_i)}+ \beta_{0}^{(t_i)}+ \epsilon,$$\end{document}

where β1(ti)\begin{document}$\beta_{1}^{(t_i)}$\end{document} is the slope, β0(ti)\begin{document}$\beta_{0}^{(t_i)}$\end{document} is the intercept and ϵ∼N(0,(σϵ(ti))2)\begin{document}$\epsilon\sim N(0,(\sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_i)})^{2})$\end{document} is the Gaussian noise.

The likelihood of the linear model is given by(9)p(y(ti)∣cti=‘‘T")=p(y(ti)∣x(ti),β0(ti),β1(ti),σϵ(ti))=∏j=1Ntip(yj(ti)∣xj(ti),β0(ti),β1(ti),σϵ(ti))=∏j=1NtiN(yj(ti)−β1(ti)xj(ti)−β0(ti)∣0,(σϵ(ti))2)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin {array}{ll} p\left(y^{(t_i)} \mid c_{t_i} = {``T"}\right) &= p\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)} \mid \boldsymbol{x}^{(t_i)}, \beta_0^{(t_i)}, \beta_1^{(t_i)}, \sigma_\epsilon^{(t_i)}\right)\\ &= \prod_{j=1}^{N_{t_i}}p\!\left(y_j^{(t_i)} \mid x_j^{(t_i)}, \beta_0^{(t_i)}, \beta_1^{(t_i)}, \sigma_\epsilon^{(t_i)}\right)\\ &= \prod_{j=1}^{N_{t_i}}{N}\left(y_j^{(t_i)} - \beta_1^{(t_i)} x_j^{(t_i)} - \beta_0^{(t_i)} \,\mid\,0, (\sigma_\epsilon^{(t_i)})^2 \right) \end{array}$$\end{document}

Similar to the inner HMM, the approximated emission probability for each data point in ith transition block is given by(10)p^(yj(ti)|gj(ti))=|y(ti)|p^(y(ti)|cti=‘‘T"),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \hat{p}(y_{j}^{(t_{i})}|g_{j}^{(t_{i})}) = |y^{(t_{i})}|\sqrt{\hat{p}(y^{(t_{i})}|c_{t_{i}} = ``T")},$$\end{document}p^(y(bi)=cbi=‘‘T")\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \hat{p}(\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}= c_{b_i}=``T")$$\end{document}

sec.title: Parameter Inference
sec.p: Due to the complexity of GPU-accelerated inference algorithm, the details are provided in Appendix 2.

The inference algorithm will infer the following parameters: (1) the hidden states g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document} of the outer HMM; (2) the hidden states of the inner HMM h\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{h}$\end{document}; (3) relevant parameters for emissions (Appendix 1—table 2) of each current signal fragment of the 2k+1\begin{document}$2k+1$\end{document} th block (base block) y(2k+1)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(2k+1)}$\end{document}, where k∈(0,1,⋯,K)\begin{document}$k\in(0,1,\cdots,K)$\end{document} and c2k+1=‘‘B"\begin{document}$c_{2k+1}=``B"$\end{document}; and (4) the linear coefficients and noise std of the 2kth\begin{document}$2k\rm th$\end{document} block (transition block) y(2k)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(2k)}$\end{document}, where k∈(1,2,⋯,K)\begin{document}$k\in(1,2,\cdots,K)$\end{document} and c2k=‘‘T"\begin{document}$c_{2k}=``T"$\end{document}.

We then integrate g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document} and h\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{h}$\end{document} into a single vector z=(z1,z2,⋯,zi,⋯,zN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{z}=(z_{1}, z_{2}, \cdots, z_{i}, \cdots, z_{N})$\end{document}, where zi=g~i⋅h~i\begin{document}$z_{i}=\tilde{g}_{i}\cdot \tilde{h}_{i}$\end{document} and g~i,h~i\begin{document}$\tilde{g}_{i}, \tilde{h}_{i}$\end{document} are the numerical mappings of gi,hi\begin{document}$g_{i},h_{i}$\end{document}. z\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{z}$\end{document} is the final output of HHMM.

sec.title: Alignment of signal segments with reference sequence
sec.p: This section describes the alignment algorithms for aligning an input fragment of signal segments μi(k)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\mu_i}^{(k)}$\end{document} and the corresponding 5-mer list si(k)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s_i}^{(k)}$\end{document}. After post-processing of the alignment path, we could get the modification states ui(k)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{u_i}^{(k)}$\end{document}. For notation simplicity, we drop the indices and denote μi(k)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\mu_i}^{(k)}$\end{document}, si(k)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s_i}^{(k)}$\end{document} and ui(k)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{u_i}^{(k)}$\end{document} by μ, s\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s}$\end{document} and u\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{u}$\end{document}, respectively. In general, one 5-mer sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document} is aligned with k\begin{document}$k$\end{document} signal segments (μi,μi+1,⋯,μi+k−1)\begin{document}$(\mu_{i}, \mu_{i+1}, \cdots, \mu_{i+k-1})$\end{document}, where k≥1\begin{document}$k\ge 1$\end{document}.

Here we discuss different scenarios of aligning sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document} with μi\begin{document}$\mu_{i}$\end{document}. The first scenario (scenario 1) is that μi\begin{document}$\mu_{i}$\end{document} is generated by unmodified 5-mer sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document}, for which we could compute the likelihood using the Normal distribution defined by (μsjun,σsjun)=Tsj,unref\begin{document}$(\mu^{un}_{s_j}, \sigma^{un}_{s_j}) = T^{ref}_{s_j, un}$\end{document}. The second scenario (scenario 2) is the μi\begin{document}$\mu_{i}$\end{document} is generated by modified 5-mer sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document}, that is N(μsjmod,σsjmod)\begin{document}$N(\mu^{mod}_{s_j}, \sigma^{mod}_{s_j})$\end{document}, where (μsjmod,σsjmod)=Tsj,modref\begin{document}$(\mu^{mod}_{s_j}, \sigma^{mod}_{s_j}) = T^{ref}_{s_j, mod}$\end{document}. The third scenario (scenario 3) is that μi\begin{document}$\mu_{i}$\end{document} is generated by an unknown nucleotide insertion event, whose probability is given by a pre-defined uniform distribution punif\begin{document}$p_{unif}$\end{document}. The fourth scenario (scenario 4) is that the 5-mer sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document} represents a deletion event on the reference sequence, whose probability is given by the same uniform distribution punif\begin{document}$p_{unif}$\end{document}. We define the scoring function as(11)f(sj,μi)={max{log(N(μi∣μsjun,(σsjun)2)),log(N(μi∣μsjmod,(σsjmod)2))},(scenario1,2),log(punif)#sj="−"orμi=0.0(scenario3,4)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle f(s_j, \mu_i) = \begin{cases} max \left\{log \left({N}\!\left(\mu_i \mid \mu_{s_j}^{{un}}, (\sigma_{s_j}^{{un}})^2\right)\right), log\left({N}\!\left(\mu_i \mid \mu_{s_j}^{{mod}}, (\sigma_{s_j}^{{mod}})^2\right)\right) \right\}, & {(scenario\,1, 2)}, \\[6pt] log(p_{{unif}})\quad {\# } s_j = "-" {or}\, \mu_i = 0.0 & {(scenario\,3, 4)} \end{cases}$$\end{document}

We can modify the classical global alignment algorithm to align μ with s\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s}$\end{document}. The log probability of aligning sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document} with μi\begin{document}$\mu_{i}$\end{document} could be used as the matching score (Equation 11) in the standard global alignment algorithm. Since one 5-mer sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document} could be aligned with multiple signal segments (μi,μi+1,⋯,μi+k−1)\begin{document}$(\mu_{i}, \mu_{i+1}, \cdots, \mu_{i+k-1})$\end{document}, we need to modify the recursion such that μi+1\begin{document}$\mu_{i+1}$\end{document} could still be aligned with sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document} even if μi\begin{document}$\mu_{i}$\end{document} is already aligned with sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document}. Therefore, we have the following recursions in our alignment algorithm (Appendix 1—figure 3a). Here we denote the scoring matrix by M\begin{document}$M$\end{document} (m×n\begin{document}$m \times n$\end{document}) and the traceback matrix by T\begin{document}$T$\end{document}, where |μ|=m−1\begin{document}$|\boldsymbol{\mu}|=m-1$\end{document} and |s|=n−1\begin{document}$|\boldsymbol{s}|=n-1$\end{document}.

caption.title: Alignment of signal segments with reference sequence.

alternatives.tex-math: \begin{document}$$\displaystyle \it {M(i,j) = max} \begin{cases} M(i-1, j-1) + f(\mu_i, s_j), & {scenario\,1, 2} \quad T(i,j) = 1 \\[6pt] M(i, j-1) + f(0, s_j), & {scenario\,3} \quad T(i,j) = 2 \\[6pt] M(i-1, j) + f(\mu_i, -), & {scenario\,4} \quad T(i,j) = 3 \\[6pt] M(i-1, j) + f(\mu_i, s_j), & {scenario\,1, 2} \quad T(i,j) = 4 \end{cases}$$\end{document}

sec.p: where both μi−1\begin{document}$\mu_{i-1}$\end{document} and μi\begin{document}$\mu_{i}$\end{document} are aligned with sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document} when T(i,j)=4\begin{document}$T(i,j)=4$\end{document}. This is the only difference between our recursion and the recursion of the standard global alignment algorithm.

Based on the proposed recursion, we developed two alignment algorithms designed for different problems: the full alignment algorithm (‘Full alignment algorithm’) allows insertions and deletions in the reference sequence, while the partial alignment algorithm (‘Partial alignment algorithm’) does not allow any insertions or deletions in the partial reference sequence. The full alignment algorithm tries to align all the current signal segments to the whole reference sequence, while the partial alignment algorithm tries to align all the current signal segments to a consecutive sub-sequence of the 5-mer list from the beginning.

The output of the alignment algorithms is a matching path between the current signal segments μ and the reference 5-mer list s\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s}$\end{document}. We denote it by γ=(μp,sq)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\gamma}= (\boldsymbol{\mu^p}, \boldsymbol{s^q})$\end{document}, where μp=(μp1,μp2,⋯,μpN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\mu^p}=(\mu_{p_1}, \mu_{p_2}, \cdots, \mu_{p_N})$\end{document} and sq=(sq1,sq2,⋯,sqN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s^q}=(s_{q_1}, s_{q_2}, \cdots, s_{q_N})$\end{document}. The length of the alignment path is given by |γ|=N\begin{document}$|\boldsymbol{\gamma}|=N$\end{document}. p=(p1,p2,⋯,pN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}=(p_{1}, p_{2}, \cdots, p_{N})$\end{document} are the indexes of the current signal segment μ in the alignment path γ\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\gamma}$\end{document}, while q=(q1,q2,⋯,qN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{q}=(q_{1}, q_{2}, \cdots, q_{N})$\end{document} are the indexes of the 5-mer list s\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s}$\end{document}. Note that there are duplicated values in p\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}$\end{document} and q\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{q}$\end{document} to allow one 5-mer to match with multiple current signal segments. Appendix 1—figure 3b provides an illustration of the alignment result.

To obtain the modification states u\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{u}$\end{document}, we will first process the optimal alignment path γ\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\gamma}$\end{document} into γ^\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\hat{\gamma}}$\end{document} (‘Post-processing of alignment path’), from which we can derive the modification states u\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{u}$\end{document} (‘Modification state estimation on single molecule level’).

sec.title: Full alignment algorithm
sec.p: The full alignment algorithm is used when the read may possess differences (insertions or deletions) compared with the reference sequence, which is the general case. The algorithm is described in Appendix 1–algorithm 1.

tr.td: Input: μ, s, TkmerOutput: Alignment path γ\begin{document}$\gamma$\end{document} // Add special symbol before μ and sμ ={0.0, μ}, s ={“–”, s} // Get the length of and μ and sm = |μ|, n = |s| // Initialize the score matrix M and traceback matrix TInitialize a m × n matrix M, with all elements set to 0.0Initialize a m × n matrix T, with all elements set to 0 // Initialize the first row of M and Tfor i ∈ {1, 2, … , n-1} do M(0, i) ← M(0, i - 1) + f (0.0, si) T(0, i)←2end// Intialize the first column of M and Tfor i ∈ {1, 2, · · · , m − 1} do M(i, 0) ← M(i − 1, 0) + f(µi , “ − ”) T(i, 0) ← 3end// Loop for all matrixfor i ∈ {1, 2,···,m − 1} do for j ∈ {1, 2,···, n − 1} do v1 ← M(i − 1, j − 1) + f(μi, sj) v2 ← M(i, j − 1) + f(0.0, sj) v3 ← M(i − 1, j) + f(μi, “ − ”) v4 ← M(i − 1, j) + f(μi, sj) M(i, j) ← max{v1, v2, v3, v4} T(i, j) ← argmax{v1, v2, v3, v4} # index of maximum endend// TracebackTraceback from position (m − 1, n − 1) to position (0, 0) using T to generate the optimalalignment path γ.

sec.title: Partial alignment algorithm
sec.p: The partial alignment algorithm is used when the read must agree with the reference sequence (no insertions or deletions). If the sequencing data is generated by in vitro transcription from a single template, all reads should agree with the reference sequence. However, the read may only match the reference sequence from the start to a middle point due to the break of the RNA molecule in the reverse transcription.

The partial alignment algorithm is a modification of the full alignment algorithm by considering this special case. Since the alignment may not be full, we traceback from the maximum value in the last row of the scoring matrix μ. This means all current signal segments must be aligned, while only the first part of the 5-mer list needs to be aligned. Appendix 1—figure 3c provides an illustration of the alignment result.

The partial alignment algorithm is described in Algorithm 1–algorithm 2. We can see that scenarios 3 and 4 have been removed from the algorithm compared with the full alignment algorithm, that is T(i,j)=2or3\begin{document}$T(i,j)=2\,or\,3$\end{document}. In other words, there should be no insertions or deletions in the 5-mer list s\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s}$\end{document}.

tr.td: Input: μ, s, TkmerOutput: Alignment path γ // Add special symbol before μ and sμ = {0.0, μ}, s = {“ − ”, s} // Get the length of μ and sm = |μ|, n = |s| // Initialize the score matrix M and traceback matrix TInitialize a m × n matrix M, with all elements set to 0.0Initialize a m × n matrix T, with all elements set to 0 // Initialize the first row of M and Tfor i ∈ {1, 2,···, n − 1} do M(0, i) ← M(0, i − 1) + f(0.0, si) T(0, i) ← 2end // Initialize the first column of M and Tfor i ∈ {1, 2,···,m − 1} do M(i, 0) ← M(i − 1, 0) + f(µi , “ − ”) T(i, 0) ← 3end // Loop for all matrixfor i ∈ {1, 2, · · · ,m − 1} do for j ∈ {1, 2, · · · , n − 1} do v1 ← M(i − 1, j − 1) + f(μi, sj) v4 ← M(i − 1, j) + f(μi, sj) M(i, j) ← max{v1, v4} if v1 ≥ v4 then T(i, j) ← 1; else T(i, j) ← 4; end endend // TracebackFind the maximum value M(m − 1, k) in the last row of the scoring matrix M.Traceback from position (m − 1, k) to position (0, 0) using T to generate the optimal alignment path γ.

sec.title: Post-processing of alignment path
sec.p: In the alignment path γ\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\gamma}$\end{document}, one k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}-mer might be aligned to noise (scenario 4) or one current signal segment may be aligned with ‘-’ (scenario 3). Firstly, we remove such pairs from the alignment path. Then, we re-compute a weighted mean for each remaining k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}-mer, since one k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}-mer may be aligned to multiple current signal segments. Here, we combine these segments and calculate the weighted mean over corresponding data points with hidden state 1, that is zi=1\begin{document}$z_{i}=1$\end{document}.

Let us assume there are m\begin{document}$m$\end{document} segments aligned to the same k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}-mer si\begin{document}$s_{i}$\end{document}, with nj\begin{document}$n_{j}$\end{document} denotes the number of data points assigned to ‘curr’ state (zi=1\begin{document}$z_{i}=1$\end{document}) in the j-th segment and μj\begin{document}$\mu_{j}$\end{document} denotes the mean of the j-th segment. The weighted mean, denoted by μ^i\begin{document}$\hat{\mu}_{i}$\end{document}, corresponding to the k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}-mer si\begin{document}$s_{i}$\end{document}, is defined as follows:(13)μ^i=∑j=1mnjn1+n2+⋯+nmμj.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \hat{\mu}_{i}= \sum^{m}_{j=1}\frac{n_{j}}{n_{1}+ n_{2}+ \cdots + n_{m}}\mu_{j}.$$\end{document}

In the end, we obtain a one-to-one correspondence between each k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}-mer and its newly derived weighted mean, denoted by μ^=(μ^1,μ^2,⋯)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\hat{\mu}}= (\hat{\mu}_{1}, \hat{\mu}_{2}, \cdots)$\end{document}. Note that the length of μ^\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\hat{\mu}}$\end{document} and s\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s}$\end{document} is the same, that is |μ^|=|s|\begin{document}$|\boldsymbol{\hat{\mu}}|=|\boldsymbol{s}|$\end{document}. We denote the post-processed alignment path by γ^=(μ^,s)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\hat{\gamma}}=(\boldsymbol{\hat{\mu}}, \boldsymbol{s})$\end{document}.

sec.title: Modification state estimation on single molecule level
sec.p: We are interested in estimating the modification state for 5-mer after alignment. Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{kmer}$\end{document} has provided the mean and std for each 5-mer, as well as its modifications. Note that Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{kmer}$\end{document} may only contain modification parameters for only a subset of 5-mers. For those 5-mers without modification parameters, their mean and std are set to ‘NA’. We could evaluate the probability density of a given 5-mer under the unmodified distribution or the modified distribution. By comparing the likelihoods, we could infer the modification state. Given the post-processed alignment path γ^=(μ^,s)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\hat{\gamma}}=(\boldsymbol{\hat{\mu}}, \boldsymbol{s})$\end{document}, we estimate the modification state of jth 5-mer sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document} by(14)uj={‘‘un"ifμsjun=‘‘NA"orσsjun=‘‘NA",‘‘un"ifN(μ^j∣μsjun,(σsjun)2)≥N(μ^j∣μsjmod,(σsjmod)2)‘‘mod"ifN(μ^j∣μsjun,(σsjun)2)<N(μ^j∣μsjmod,(σsjmod)2)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle u_j = \begin{cases} ``un" & {if}\, \mu_{s_j}^{{un}} = ``NA" or\, \sigma_{s_j}^{un} = ``NA", \\[6pt] ``un" & {if} \, {N}\!\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid \mu_{s_j}^{un}, (\sigma_{s_j}^{un})^2\right) \ge N\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid \mu_{s_j}^{{mod}}, (\sigma_{s_j}^{mod})^2\right) \\[6pt] {``mod"} & {if}\,{N}\!\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid \mu_{s_j}^{{un}}, (\sigma_{s_j}^{{un}})^2\right) \lt {N}\!\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid \mu_{s_j}^{{mod}}, (\sigma_{s_j}^{{mod}})^2\right) \end{cases}$$\end{document}

where (μsjun,σsjun)=Tsj,unkmer\begin{document}$(\mu^{un}_{s_j}, \sigma^{un}_{s_j}) = T^{kmer}_{s_j, un}$\end{document} and (μsjmod,σsjmod)=Tsj,modkmer\begin{document}$(\mu^{mod}_{s_j}, \sigma^{mod}_{s_j}) = T^{kmer}_{s_j, mod}$\end{document}.

In the end, we get the modification state vector u=(u1,u2,⋯,ui,⋯,u|γ^|)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{u}=(u_{1}, u_{2}, \cdots, u_{i}, \cdots, u_{|\boldsymbol{\hat{\gamma}}|})$\end{document} for each 5-mer in the post-processed alignment path γ^\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\hat{\gamma}}$\end{document}.

sec.title: Modification probability estimation on single molecule level
sec.p: In addition to estimating the modification state, it is also crucial to determine the modification probability of each 5-mer on each read, which is essential for accurately benchmarking the performance at the single-molecule level.

Given the post-processed alignment path γ^=(μ^,s)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\hat{\gamma}}=(\boldsymbol{\hat{\mu}}, \boldsymbol{s})$\end{document}, we estimate the modification probability of jth 5-mer sj\begin{document}$s_{j}$\end{document} by(15)pj={‘‘NA"ifμsjun=‘‘NA" or σsjun=‘‘NA"p(μ^j∣Tsj,modkmer)p(μ^j∣Tsj,modkmer)+p(μ^j∣Tsj,unkmer),ifμsjun≠‘‘NA" and σsjun≠‘‘NA"\begin{document}$$\displaystyle p_j = \begin{cases} ``NA" & if\, \mu_{s_j}^{{un}} = ``NA" \ or\ \sigma_{s_j}^{un} = ``NA" \\[8pt] \dfrac{ p\!\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid T^{kmer}_{s_j,{mod}}\right) }{ p\!\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid T^{kmer}_{s_j,{mod}}\right) + p\!\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid T^{kmer}_{s_j,{un}}\right) }, & {if}\, \mu_{s_j}^{un} \neq ``NA" \ \text{and}\ \sigma_{s_j}^{un} \neq ``NA" \end{cases} $$\end{document}

where (μsjun,σsjun)=Tsj,unkmer\begin{document}$(\mu^{un}_{s_j}, \sigma^{un}_{s_j}) = T^{kmer}_{s_j, un}$\end{document} and (μsjmod,σsjmod)=Tsj,modkmer\begin{document}$(\mu^{mod}_{s_j}, \sigma^{mod}_{s_j}) = T^{kmer}_{s_j, mod}$\end{document}. Indeed, the pj\begin{document}$p_{j}$\end{document} is the posterior probability given the distribution of modified state:(16)pj=p(Tsj,modkmer∣μ^j)=p(μ^j∣Tsj,modkmer)⋅p(Tsj,modkmer)p(μ^j∣Tsj,modkmer)⋅p(Tsj,modkmer)+p(μ^j∣Tsj,unkmer)⋅p(Tsj,unkmer)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle p_j = p\!\left(T^{{kmer}}_{sj,{mod}} \mid \hat{\mu}_j\right) = \frac{ p\!\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid T^{kmer}_{sj,{mod}}\right)\,\cdot p\!\left(T^{{kmer}}_{sj,{mod}}\right) }{ p\!\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid T^{kmer}_{sj,{mod}}\right)\,\cdot p\!\left(T^{kmer}_{sj,{mod}}\right) + p\!\left(\hat{\mu}_j \mid T^{{kmer}}_{sj,{un}}\right)\,\cdot p\!\left(T^{{kmer}}_{sj,{un}}\right) }$$\end{document}
where we assume that the prior probabilities are equal, p(Tsj,modkmer)=p(Tsj,unkmer)\begin{document}$p(T^{{kmer}}_{sj,{mod}}) =p(T^{{kmer}}_{sj,{un}})$\end{document}.

In the end, we get the modification probability vector p=(p1,p2,⋯,pi,⋯,p|γ^|)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}=(p_{1}, p_{2}, \cdots, p_{i}, \cdots, p_{|\boldsymbol{\hat{\gamma}}|})$\end{document} for each 5-mer in the post-processed alignment path γ^\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\hat{\gamma}}$\end{document}.

sec.title: Modification rate estimation on site level
sec.p: In real data analysis, we need to decide the modification rate of a genomic location, which quantifies the probability of a read mapped to this location to be in the modified state. For a genomic location, we denote the 5-mer from reads mapped to this location as (s1,s2,⋯,sN)\begin{document}$(s_{1}, s_{2}, \cdots, s_{N})$\end{document}. Note that all si,i=1,2,⋯,N\begin{document}$s_{i}, i=1,2,\cdots,N$\end{document} are identical 5-mers on different reads. The modification state of these 5-mers on different reads is denoted by (u1,u2,⋯,uN)\begin{document}$(u_{1}, u_{2}, \cdots, u_{N})$\end{document}. The modification rate is given by(17)η=∑i=1Nui=‘‘mod"N\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}\eta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}u_{i}=``mod"}{N}\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

sec.title: Estimation of k-mer parameters table
sec.p: This section provides the inference algorithms for 5-mer parameter table Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{kmer}$\end{document}. This is necessary for training data, as we do not have accurate prior parameters for Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{kmer}$\end{document}.

Given input data Y=(y1,y2,⋯,yi,⋯,yN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{Y}=(\boldsymbol{y_1}, \boldsymbol{y_2},\cdots, \boldsymbol{y_i}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y_N})$\end{document}, we first perform base calling and mapping to get S=(s1,s2,⋯,si,⋯,sN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{S}=(\boldsymbol{s_1}, \boldsymbol{s_2},\cdots, \boldsymbol{s_i}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{s_N})$\end{document} run HHMM for all reads such we get M=(μ1,μ2,⋯,μi,⋯,μN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{M}=(\boldsymbol{\mu_1}, \boldsymbol{\mu_2},\cdots, \boldsymbol{\mu_i}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{\mu_N})$\end{document}. We assume there are ni\begin{document}$n_{i}$\end{document} fragments for the ith read yi=(yi(1),yi(2),⋯,yi(ni))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y_i}=(\boldsymbol{y_i}^{(1)}, \boldsymbol{y_i}^{(2)},\cdots,\boldsymbol{y_i}^{(n_i)})$\end{document}. Similarly, we have si=(si(1),si(2),⋯,si(ni))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{s_i}=(\boldsymbol{s_i}^{(1)}, \boldsymbol{s_i}^{(2)},\cdots,\boldsymbol{s_i}^{(n_i)})$\end{document} and μi=(μi(1),μi(2),⋯,μi(ni))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\mu_i}=(\boldsymbol{\mu_i}^{(1)}, \boldsymbol{\mu_i}^{(2)},\cdots,\boldsymbol{\mu_i}^{(n_i)})$\end{document}.

The inference algorithm (Appendix 1–algorithm 3) then estimate Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{kmer}$\end{document} given S\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{S}$\end{document}, M\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{M}$\end{document} and Tref\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{ref}$\end{document} (https://github.com/nanoporetech/kmer_models; Brennen, 2023). As illustrated in Appendix 1—figure 4, the inference algorithm infers the parameters iteratively. With a given Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{kmer}$\end{document}, we can use it to obtain the pairing between raw current signal segments and 5-mers, as well as modification states, through the alignment algorithm (‘Alignment of signal segments with reference sequence’). Given this pairing, we extract all current signal segments for a specific 5-mer, which may correspond to different genomic locations and different reads. As there are two states for this 5-mer (unmodified and modified), we expect to observe two peaks in the density of the current signal segments. Therefore, we could fit a two-component Gaussian mixture model to the current signal segments to estimate relevant parameters of the given 5-mer in Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{kmer}$\end{document}.

caption.title: Estimation of 5-mer parameters.
caption.p: Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{kmer}$\end{document} is initialized using Tref\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{ref}$\end{document}. In training, our algorithm infers the parameters iteratively. After training, Tref\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{ref}$\end{document} is fixed in testing.

tr.th: Appendix 1–algorithm 3. Inference algorithm of k-mer parameter table.

tr.td: Input: S, M, TrefOutput:Tkmer // Initialize Tkmer, ∀s ∈ {AAAAA,AAAAC, · · · , TTTTT}, ∀u ∈ {“un”, “mod”}for (μs,u, σs,u) ∈ Tkmer do (μs,u, σs,u) = Trefs,“un”end // iterate T times to update Tkmerfor round = 1, 2 · · · T do // align all fragments of all reads Perform full alignment algorithm (Appendix 1–algorithm 1) for μi(k) and si(k) to get the alignment path γi(k) , where i = 1 · · · N and k = 1 · · · ni. Post-process (Post-processing of alignment path) the alignment path γi(k) into γ^i(k)\begin{document}$\hat {\gamma}^{(k)}_{i}$\end{document} = [(μ^1(k),s1(k)),(μ^2(k),s2(k)),⋯]\begin{document}$[(\hat {\mu}_{1}^{(k)},s_{1}^{(k)}),(\hat {\mu}_{2}^{(k)},s_{2}^{(k)}),\cdots]$\end{document}. Concatenate all γ^i(k)\begin{document}$\hat{\gamma}_{i}^{(k)} $\end{document} and re-index into one vector Γ^=[(μ^,s1),(μ^,s2),⋯]\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol \Gamma }=[(\hat {\mu},s_{1}),(\hat {\mu},s_{2}),\cdots]$\end{document}. // parameter estimation for s0 ∈ {AAAAA,AAAAC, · · · , TTTTT} do Collect all μ^j\begin{document}$\hat{\mu}_{j}$\end{document} in Γ^\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol \Gamma}$\end{document} where sj = s0 into one vector x={μ^j,∀sj=s0,∀(μ^j,sj)}∈Γ^\begin{document}$x=\{\hat{\mu}_{j},\,\forall s_{j}=s_{0},\,\forall (\hat{\mu}_{j},\, s_{j}) \}\in \hat{\boldsymbol \Gamma}$\end{document}. Fit x using a two-component Gaussian Mixture Model with the first component mean fixed to μ20\begin{document}$\mu_{2_{0}}$\end{document}, where (μ20\begin{document}$\mu_{2_{0}}$\end{document}, σ20\begin{document}$\sigma_{2_{0}}$\end{document}) =Ts0,‘‘un"ref\begin{document}$T_{s_{0},``un"}^{ref}$\end{document}, and obtain the std of the first component σ1, the mean and std of the second component μ2, σ2. Update the parameters of 5-mer s0 in Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{kmer} $\end{document} : Ts0,‘‘un"kmer←(μs0,σ1)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol T^{kmer}_{s_{0},``un"}\leftarrow (\mu_{s_{0},\sigma _{1}})$\end{document} and Ts0,``mod"kmer←(μ2,σ2)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol T^{{kmer}}_{s_0,\text{``mod"}} \leftarrow (\mu_2, \sigma_2)$\end{document} end Update Tkmer manually based on several heuristic criteria.end

sec.p: In practice, the following heuristic criteria are used: (1) for a given 5-mer, perform GMM for each genomic location with the same 5-mer and only pool those sites with high modification rate (2) there must be two peaks in the density plot of a given 5-mer; (3) the mean of the two peaks are far apart; (4) the weight of second component (modified state) must be large, e.g. >0.1; (5) the weight of the first component (unmodified state) should be larger than the second component (modified state), as majority nucleotides are unmodified; (6) limit the size of std for both components in the GMM (σ1<5\begin{document}$\sigma_{1}\lt5$\end{document}, σ2<5\begin{document}$\sigma_{2}\lt5$\end{document}) to comply with the physical settings.

In the end, we will get an updated Tkmer\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{T}^{{kmer}}$\end{document} from the training data, which contains the modification parameters for the strongest 5-mers. As illustrated in Appendix 1—table 1, we do have parameters for the modification state for certain k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}-mers such as ‘AAACA’, ‘AAACT’ etc.

sec.title: Evaluation of segmentation and event alignment
sec.p: The output of SegPore eventalign is the alignment path γ = (μ, s), which represents a one-to-one correspondence between the mean list μ = (μ1, μ2,...) and the k-mer lists = (s1, s2, ...). In addition, the corresponding standard deviation list σ = (σ1, σ2, ...) is also obtained along the alignment path. Appendix 1—table 3 is an example of the SegPore eventalign output, which illustrates one alignment path. The column ref_kmer corresponds to s, the column mean corresponds to μ, and the column stdv corresponds to σ.

caption.title: SegPore eventalign output example.

sec.p: Given the eventalign results, we use two metrics to evaluate the performance of the segmentation and event alignment: (1) the average std σ^\begin{document}$\hat{\sigma}$\end{document}, and (2) the average log-likelihood L^\begin{document}$\hat{L}$\end{document}. Assuming there are N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} reads in total (each read has one path), and the nth read has Kn\begin{document}$K_{n}$\end{document} events (|γn|=Kn\begin{document}$|\boldsymbol{\gamma}_{n}|= K_{n}$\end{document}). The average std σ^\begin{document}$\hat{\sigma}$\end{document} is defined as(18)σ^=1N∑n=1N{1Kn∑k=1Knσk},\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \hat{\sigma} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \left\{\frac{1}{K_n}\sum_{k=1}^{K_n}\sigma_k \right\},$$\end{document}

and the average log-likelihood L^\begin{document}$\hat{L}$\end{document} is defined as(19)L^=1N∑n=1N{1Kn∑k=1Knlog⁡N(μk|μskref,σskref)}\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \hat{L}= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\Big \{ \frac{1}{K_{n}}\sum_{k=1}^{K_{n}}\log \mathcal{N}\Big(\mu_{k}| \mu^{ref}_{s_k}, \sigma^{ref}_{s_k}\Big) \Big\}$$\end{document}
where (μskref,σskref)=Tskref\begin{document}$(\mu^{ref}_{s_k}, \sigma^{ref}_{s_k}) = T^{ref}_{s_k}$\end{document}.

As shown in Appendix 1—figure 5, the red line represents the event mean μk\begin{document}$\mu_{k}$\end{document} and the shaded area represents the std σskest\begin{document}$\sigma^{est}_{s_k}$\end{document} for event k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}. A poorly segmented raw signal corresponding to an event will exhibit a large standard deviation. Therefore, a smaller σ^\begin{document}$\hat{\sigma}$\end{document} indicates lower variations within the raw signal segment, signifying better performance.

caption.title: Illustration of segmentation and event alignment.

sec.p: If an event is aligned to the correct reference k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}-mer, the mean μk\begin{document}$\mu_{k}$\end{document} will be close to the reference μskref\begin{document}$\mu^{ref}_{s_k}$\end{document} and the log-likelihood L^\begin{document}$\hat{L}$\end{document} will be large. So higher L^\begin{document}$\hat{L}$\end{document} means more similar results to ONT’s estimates and better performances.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: We have introduced the hierarchical hidden Markov model (HHMM) in Appendix 1. The HHMM is a two-layer HMM, consisting of the outer HMM and the inner HMM. The outer HMM specifies the base blocks and transition blocks. The inner HMM specifies the base blocks. Linear models are used for the transition blocks. However, we have not provided the details for the parameter inference. This document aims to provide a comprehensive introduction to the inference algorithm.

As we know, the raw fast5 files are very large, which contain millions of reads. Each read has 20,000 measurements in the raw current signal. This poses a significant computational burden to the HHMM inference algorithm. Therefore, we used GPU to accelerate the inference algorithm.
We will first introduce the general inference algorithm, then describe the implementation details.

For simplicity, we assume the input is a raw current signal of a single read with N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} measurements y=(y1,y2,⋯,yN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}= (y_{1}, y_{2}, \cdots, y_{N})$\end{document}. The output is the hidden states of the outer HMM g=(g1,g2,⋯,gN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}= (g_{1}, g_{2}, \cdots, g_{N})$\end{document} and the hidden states of the inner HMM h=(h1,h2,⋯,hN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{h}= (h_{1}, h_{2}, \cdots, h_{N})$\end{document}, where gi∈{‘‘B",‘‘T"}\begin{document}$g_{i}\in\{``B",``T"\}$\end{document} and hi∈{‘‘curr",‘‘prev",‘‘next",‘‘noise"}\begin{document}$h_{i}\in \{``curr", ``prev", ``next", ``noise" \}$\end{document}.

Given a hidden state configuration of the outer HMM g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document}, it partitions the raw current signal y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document} into alternating base blocks and transition blocks c=(c1,c2,⋯,ci,⋯,c2K+1)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{c}= (c_{1}, c_{2}, \cdots, c_{i}, \cdots, c_{2K+1})$\end{document}, where ci∈{‘‘B",‘‘T"}\begin{document}$c_{i}\in \{``B", ``T"\}$\end{document} denotes the label of ith block. Note that ci=‘‘B"∀i=1,3,⋯,2K+1\begin{document}$c_{i}=``B"\: \forall i=1,3,\cdots,2K+1$\end{document} and ci=‘‘T"∀i=2,4,⋯,2K\begin{document}$c_{i}=``T" \: \forall i=2,4,\cdots,2K$\end{document}. Now we can denote y=(y(1),y(2),⋯,y(2K+1))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}=(\boldsymbol{y^{(1)}}, \boldsymbol{y^{(2)}}, \cdots,\boldsymbol{y^{(2K+1)}})$\end{document}.

The general idea of the inference algorithm is that we will calculate the likelihood for all possible configurations of the hidden states of the outer HMM g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document} and choose the one with the largest likelihood as our final estimation. Due to the special constraints of g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document}, we can enumerate all possible configurations (‘Enumeration of hidden states of outer HMM’). Here we denote the full configuration set of g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document} by G\begin{document}$\mathcal{G}$\end{document}. The general inference algorithm is discussed in detail in ‘General inference algorithm’.

The general inference algorithm provides the analytic forms for calculating the exact likelihood. However, a direct implementation of the algorithm (Python and C++) requires huge amounts of computation resources and time to handle a typical fast5 file. Therefore, we implement a GPU version of the inference algorithm (‘Implementation details’), in which base blocks from different configurations are simultaneously inferred on different GPU cores.

sec.title: Enumeration of hidden states of outer HMM
sec.p: This section describes how to get the full configuration set G=(g1,g2,⋯,gi,⋯,gM)\begin{document}$\mathcal{G}= (\boldsymbol{g}_{1}, \boldsymbol{g}_{2}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{g}_{i}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{g}_{M})$\end{document} given the raw signal y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document}. Obviously, it is not possible to enumerate all hidden state gi=(gi1,gi2,⋯,gij,⋯,giN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}_{i}= (g_{i1}, g_{i2}, \cdots, g_{ij}, \cdots,g_{iN})$\end{document} (where gij∈{‘‘B",‘‘T"}\begin{document}$g_{ij}\in\{``B",``T"\}$\end{document}) because there are 2N\begin{document}$2^{N}$\end{document} configurations and N\begin{document}$N$\end{document} can be larger than 20,000. However, there exist special properties of the raw signal y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document}: (1) the signal should be in alternating base and transition blocks (2) the hidden state gi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}_{i}$\end{document} does not change within a block (3) the average length of base blocks is larger than that of the transition blocks (4) the variation of base blocks is smaller than that of transition blocks. Based on these data properties, we could partition the raw signal y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document} into atomic level base and transition blocks (‘Atomic block initialization’). Multiple consecutive atomic level base and transition blocks can be merged into a single larger base block. As shown in Appendix 2—figure 1a, we partition the raw signal y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document} into 15 atomic-level blocks (B1,T1,B2,T2,⋯,B7,T7,B8)\begin{document}$(B1, T1, B2, T2, \cdots, B7, T7, B8)$\end{document}. We can merge (B1,T1,B2)\begin{document}$(B1, T1, B2)$\end{document} into a new base block or (B5,T5,B6,T6,B7)\begin{document}$(B5, T5, B6, T6, B7)$\end{document} into a new base block (Appendix 2—figure 1b).

Given a pre-defined partition g0\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}_{0}$\end{document} of raw signal y\begin{document}$y$\end{document}, we can derive the 2K+1\begin{document}$2K+1$\end{document} atomic blocks

(B1,T1,B2,T2,⋯,BK,TK,BK+1))\begin{document}$(B_{1}, T_{1}, B_{2}, T2, \cdots, B_{K}, T_{K}, B_{K+1}))$\end{document}. To facilitate the description of the enumeration algorithm, we define seg(i,j)=(Bi+1,Ti+1,Bi+2,⋯,Tj−1,Bj)\begin{document}$seg(i,j)=(B_{i+1}, T_{i+1}, B_{i+2},\cdots, T_{j-1}, B_{j})$\end{document} to be the merged block of all blocks in seg(i,j)\begin{document}$seg(i,j)$\end{document}, where 0≤i<j≤K+1\begin{document}$0\le i \lt j \le K+1$\end{document}. A path is then a list of non-overlapping and consecutive blocks. For example, the path corresponding to gi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}_{i}$\end{document} in Appendix 2—figure 1b is given by [seg(0,2),T2,B3,T3,B4,T4,seg(4,7),T7,B8]\begin{document}$[seg(0,2), T_{2}, B_{3}, T_{3}, B_{4},T_{4},seg(4,7), T_{7}, B_{8}]$\end{document}. To reduce the number of paths, we set the maximum number of base blocks in any merged block to Nmax\begin{document}$N_{max}$\end{document}. The enumeration algorithm is given by Appendix 2–algorithm 1.

tr.th: Appendix 2–algorithm 1. Enumeration of hidden states of outer HMM.

tr.td: Input: g0, Nmax, KOutput: G\begin{document}$\mathcal{G} $\end{document}Derive (B1, T1, B2, T2,··· ,BK, TK, BK+1)) from g0G←{}\begin{document}$\mathcal{G}\leftarrow \{\}$\end{document}for path in recur_enum(0) do Convert path to g Append g toG\begin{document}$\mathcal{G}$\end{document}end // Recursive function to enumerate all paths starting from position stFunction recur_enum(st): res = [] if st = K then return [BK+1] end // “[” is inclusive and “)” is exclusive for i ∈ [st, K + 1) do if i − st > Nmax then return res end tmp_seg = seg(st, i) all_paths_from_i = recur_enum(i) for path in all_ paths_ from_i do tmp_path = concatenate tmp_seg with path Append tmp_path to res end end return res;

sec.title: General inference algorithm
sec.p: Given all possible hidden state configurations of the outer HMM G\begin{document}$\mathcal{G}$\end{document}, we want to calculate the likelihood for all g∈G\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}\in \mathcal{G}$\end{document}. In order to calculate the likelihood, we need to specify the parameters for the emission distributions.

Given g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document}, we can derive that y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document} is divided into 2K+1\begin{document}$2K+1$\end{document} alternating base and transition blocks. We denote all blocks by y=(y(1),y(2),⋯,y(i),⋯,y(2K+1))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}=(\boldsymbol{y}^{(1)}, \boldsymbol{y}^{(2)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(i)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(2K+1)})$\end{document}, where y(i)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(i)}$\end{document} represent the ith block. The base blocks are given by (y(b1),y(b2),⋯,y(bi),⋯,y(bK+1))\begin{document}$(\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_1)}, \boldsymbol{y}^{(b_2)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(b_{K+1})})$\end{document}, where bi=2i−1\begin{document}$b_{i}=2i-1$\end{document} and i∈{1,2,⋯,K+1}\begin{document}$i\in \{1, 2, \cdots, K+1\}$\end{document}. The transition blocks are given by (y(t1),y(t2),⋯,y(ti),⋯,y(tK))\begin{document}$(\boldsymbol{y}^{(t_1)}, \boldsymbol{y}^{(t_2)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{y}^{(t_{K})})$\end{document}, where ti=2i\begin{document}$t_{i}=2i$\end{document} and i∈{1,2,⋯,K}\begin{document}$i\in \{1, 2, \cdots, K\}$\end{document}.

We pre-specify the transition probabilities Touter={TBBouter,TBTouter,TTBouter,TTTouter}\begin{document}$T^{outer}= \{ T^{outer}_{BB}, T^{outer}_{BT}, T^{outer}_{TB}, T^{outer}_{TT}\}$\end{document} between the base state and the transition state in the outer HMM, as shown in Appendix 2—table 1. The pre-specification has two considerations: (1) the probability of a transition from B to T after 10 consecutive B states is 0.1; (2) the probability of a transition from T to B after 10 consecutive T states is larger than 0.9; (3) to reduce computation load in the parameter inference. We pre-specify the initial probability of the first hidden state to 1, since g\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{g}$\end{document} always starts with a ‘B’ state.

caption.title: Transition parameters of the outer HMM (Touter\begin{document}$T^{outer}$\end{document}).

sec.p: For inner HMM, we pre-specify the transition probabilities Tinner\begin{document}$T^{inner}$\end{document} shown in Appendix 2—table 2, which is based on the following consideration (1) large proportion of the data should come from the ‘curr’ state, that is it is easy to transit from other states to ‘curr’ state; (2) it is unlikely to transit from ‘prev’ to ‘next’, and vice versa; (3) it is unlikely to transit from other states to the ‘noise’ state. We pre-specify the initial probability of the first hidden state to 0.25, which gives equal probabilities for all four states. We pre-specify the parameters of the ‘noise’ state (Unif(lb,ub)\begin{document}$Unif(lb, ub)$\end{document}) to lb=50\begin{document}$lb=50$\end{document} and ub=130\begin{document}$ub=130$\end{document}, which are estimated from the current signals across different datasets to represent a reasonable range.

caption.title: Transition parameters of the inner HMM (Tinner\begin{document}$T^{inner}$\end{document}).

sec.p: The remaining parameters, mainly associated with emission distributions, need to be estimated. For base blocks, we have inner HMM parameters ϕ=(ϕ(b1),ϕ(b2),⋯,ϕ(bK+1))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\phi}= (\phi^{(b_1)}, \phi^{(b_2)}, \cdots,\phi^{(b_{K+1})})$\end{document} and σ=(σ(b1),σ(b2),⋯,σ(bK+1))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\sigma}= (\sigma^{(b_1)}, \sigma^{(b_2)}, \cdots,\sigma^{(b_{K+1})})$\end{document}. For transition blocks, we have linear coefficients β0=(β0(t1),β0(t2),⋯,β0(tK))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\beta}_{0}= (\beta_{0}^{(t_1)}, \beta_{0}^{(t_2)}, \cdots,\beta_{0}^{(t_K)})$\end{document}, β1=(β1(t1),β1(t2),⋯,β1(tK))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\beta}_{1}= (\beta_{1}^{(t_1)}, \beta_{1}^{(t_2)}, \cdots,\beta_{1}^{(t_K)})$\end{document} and noise std σϵ=(σϵ(t1),σϵ(t2),⋯,σϵ(tK))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\sigma_{\epsilon}}= (\sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_1)}, \sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_2)}, \cdots,\sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_K)})$\end{document}.

The general idea of the inference algorithm is to calculate the joint likelihood of the outer HMM given for any g\begin{document}$g$\end{document}. However, it is not possible to calculate the emission probability for each measurement as there exist dependencies with a base or transition block. Here we first calculate the joint likelihood for a base block (inner HMM) or a transition block (linear model), then average the joint likelihood to each measurement to use it as substitute for the emission probability of each measurement in the outer HMM. Here we use ω=(ω1,ω2,⋯,ωN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\omega}= (\omega_{1}, \omega_{2}, \cdots, \omega_{N})$\end{document} to denote the approximated emission probability of each measurement. Note that ωi\begin{document}$\omega_{i}$\end{document} is derived either based on the inner HMM or the linear model, and |ω|=|y|=|g|=|h|\begin{document}$|\boldsymbol{\omega}|=|\boldsymbol{y}|=|\boldsymbol{g}|=|\boldsymbol{h}|$\end{document}.
The general inference algorithm is given by Appendix 2–algorithm 2.

tr.th: Appendix 2–algorithm 2. General inference algorithm for HHMM.

tr.td: Input: y, G\begin{document}$\mathcal{G}$\end{document}, Touter, πouter, Tinner, πinner,Output: best path g~\begin{document}$\tilde {g}$\end{document} of outer HMM, best path h~\begin{document}$\tilde {\boldsymbol h}$\end{document} of inner HMM, ϕ, σ, β0, β1, σϵ // Initializationset all elements of ω~,g~andh~\begin{document}$\tilde {\boldsymbol \omega} ,\tilde {\boldsymbol g}\, {\rm and}\, \tilde {\boldsymbol h}$\end{document} to 0, and ∣g~∣=∣h~∣=∣y∣\begin{document}$\mid \tilde {\boldsymbol g}\mid =\mid \tilde {\boldsymbol h}\mid =\mid \boldsymbol y\mid$\end{document}H←{}\begin{document}$\mathcal{H} \leftarrow \{\}$\end{document}Γ ← {} // Calculate likelihood for each gfor g ∈ G\begin{document}$\mathcal{G}$\end{document} do h→h~\begin{document}${\boldsymbol h} \rightarrow \tilde {\boldsymbol h}$\end{document} Divide y into base and transition blocks (y(1), y(2), · · · , y(2K+1)) based on g // transition state parameter estimation Estimate ω^(ti),β^0(ti),β^1(ti),σϵ(ti)\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol \omega }^{(t_{i})},\,\hat {\beta }^{(t_{i})}_{0},\,\hat {\beta }^{(t_{i})}_{1},\sigma _{\epsilon }^{(t_{i})}$\end{document} using linear model (Appendix 2–algorithm 3), where ti = 2i and i ∈ {1, 2,···, K} Update relevant part of ω using ω^(ti)\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol \omega }^{(t_{i})}$\end{document}. // base state parameter estimation Initialize ϕ(bi) and σ(bi) use the empirical mean and std of y(bi), where bi = 2i − 1 and i ∈ {1, 2,···, K + 1} ϕnew ← ϕ σnew ← σ for round = 1, 2,···, R do for i ∈ (1, 2,···, K + 1) do bi ← 2i − 1 // inner HMM parameter estimation Estimate ω^(bi), h^(bi), ϕ^(bi), σ^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol \omega}^{(b_i)},\ \hat{\boldsymbol h}^{(b_i)},\ \hat{\phi}^{(b_i)},\ \hat{\sigma}^{(b_i)}$\end{document} using Forward-backward algorithm (Appendix 2–algorithm 4) given y(bi), ϕ(bi), σ(bi), ϕ(bi−1), σ(bi−1), ϕ(bi+1), σ(bi+1), Tinner, πinner\begin{document}$\boldsymbol y^{(b_i)},\ \phi^{(b_i)},\ \sigma^{(b_i)},\ \phi^{(b_{i-1})},\ \sigma^{(b_{i-1})},\ \phi^{(b_{i+1})},\ \sigma^{(b_{i+1})},\ T^{\text{inner}},\ \boldsymbol \pi^{\text{inner}}$\end{document} Update corresponding elements of ϕnew and σnew using ϕ^(bi),σ^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat {\phi }^{(b_{i})},\hat {\sigma}^{(b_{i})}$\end{document} Update relevant part of h and ω using h^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol h}^{(b_{i})}$\end{document} and ω^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol \omega }^{(b_{i})}$\end{document} end ϕ ← ϕnew σ ← σnew end Calculate the joint likelihood of the outer HMM using g,ω,Touter,πouter\begin{document}$g,\,\omega ,T^{outer},\pi ^{outer}$\end{document} γ=πg1outer∏i=2NTgi−1giouter∏j=1Nωj\begin{document}$\gamma = \pi_{g_1}^{\text{outer}} \prod_{i=2}^{N} T_{g_{i-1} g_i}^{\text{outer}} \prod_{j=1}^{N} \omega_j$\end{document} Append h and γ to H\begin{document}$\mathcal{H}$\end{document} and Γ, respectively.endChoose the largest likelihood from Γ and retrieve the corresponding g and h from G\begin{document}$\mathcal{G}$\end{document} and H\begin{document}$\mathcal{H}$\end{document}, respectively.g~←g\begin{document}$\tilde {\boldsymbol g}\leftarrow {\boldsymbol g}$\end{document}h~←h\begin{document}$\tilde {\boldsymbol h}\leftarrow {\boldsymbol h}$\end{document}

sec.title: Atomic block initialization
sec.p: As mentioned in ‘Enumeration of hidden states of outer HMM’, we need to partition the raw signal y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document} into 2K+1\begin{document}$2K+1$\end{document} atomic blocks

(B1,T1,B2,T2,⋯,BK,TK,BK+1)\begin{document}$(B_{1}, T_{1}, B_{2}, T2, \cdots, B_{K}, T_{K}, B_{K+1})$\end{document}. If we can identify all atomic transition blocks (T1,T2,⋯,TK)\begin{document}$(T_{1}, T_{2}, \cdots, T_{K})$\end{document}, then the atomic base blocks are automatically determined.

We assume atomic transition blocks (T1,T2,⋯,TK)\begin{document}$(T_{1}, T_{2}, \cdots, T_{K})$\end{document} have higher variations than atomic base blocks. If we fit a line to a transition block, the absolute value of the slope of the line should be large. Based on this idea, we can obtain the atomic transition blocks as follows:

list-item.p: For each measurement i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}, fit another line to (yi−1,yi,yi+1)\begin{document}$(y_{i-1},y_{i},y_{i+1})$\end{document} and obtain the slope β1\begin{document}$\beta_{1}$\end{document}, fit a line to (yi−2,yi−1,yi,yi+1,yi+2)\begin{document}$(y_{i-2},y_{i-1},y_{i},y_{i+1},y_{i+2})$\end{document} and obtain the slope β2\begin{document}$\beta_{2}$\end{document}, use βi=|0.5∗(β1+β2)|\begin{document}$\beta_{i}= |0.5*(\beta_{1}+ \beta_{2})|$\end{document} as the absolute slope for yi\begin{document}$y_{i}$\end{document}.

Smooth the obtain slopes β=(β1,β2,⋯,βN)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\beta}=(\beta_{1}, \beta_{2}, \cdots, \beta_{N})$\end{document} by taking the mean of a sliding window of size. Now we get the smoothed β¯\begin{document}$\bar{{\boldsymbol{\beta}}}$\end{document}

Find peaks from β¯\begin{document}$\overline{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$\end{document} using the find_peaks() function in the Python Scipy.signal package. Here, set the minimum distance of detected peaks to 10. Now we get a list of peak positions θ=(θ1,θ2,⋯,θK)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\theta}= (\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \cdots, \theta_{K})$\end{document}.
Expand θk\begin{document}$\theta_{k}$\end{document} to the atomic transition block Tk\begin{document}$T_{k}$\end{document}. We fit a 3-segment spline to

(yθk−8,⋯,yθk−l,⋯,yθk,⋯,yθk+r,⋯,yθk+8)\begin{document}$(y_{\theta_k - 8}, \cdots, y_{\theta_k - l}, \cdots, y_{\theta_k}, \cdots, y_{\theta_k + r}, \cdots, y_{\theta_k + 8})$\end{document}, where 0<l,r<8\begin{document}$0\ltl,r\lt8$\end{document}. The slopes of the first segment (yθk−8,⋯,yθk−l−1)\begin{document}$(y_{\theta_k - 8}, \cdots, y_{\theta_k - l - 1})$\end{document} and the third segment (yθk+r+1,⋯,yθk+8)\begin{document}$(y_{\theta_k + r + 1}, \cdots, y_{\theta_k + 8})$\end{document} are set to 0, while a standard linear model is fit to the second segment (yθk−l,⋯,yθk,⋯,yθk+r)\begin{document}$(y_{\theta_k - l}, \cdots, y_{\theta_k}, \cdots, y_{\theta_k + r})$\end{document}. In the fitting, the slope of the second segment should be larger than 5.0. After the fitting of the spline, we take the residuals and calculate the joint likelihood assuming each residual follows a Gaussian distribution N(0,4)\begin{document}$N(0, 4)$\end{document}. By enumerating all l,r\begin{document}$l,r$\end{document}, we pick the l^,r^\begin{document}$\hat{l},\hat{r}$\end{document} with the largest joint likelihood. Therefore, the final kth transition block Tk\begin{document}$T_{k}$\end{document} corresponding to the interval [θk−l,θk+r]\begin{document}$[\theta_{k}-l, \theta_{k}+r]$\end{document}.

sec.title: Emission probabilities of linear model
sec.p: For the ith transition block, the measurements are given by y(ti)=(y1(ti),y2(ti),⋯,yNti(ti))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)}=(y_{1}^{(t_i)},y_{2}^{(t_i)},\cdots, y_{N_{t_i}}^{(t_i)})$\end{document}, where ti=2i\begin{document}$t_{i}=2i$\end{document} and i∈1,2,⋯,K\begin{document}$i\in{1,2,\cdots,K}$\end{document}. We denote the corresponding index of y(ti)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)}$\end{document} by x(ti)=(1,2,⋯,Nti)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{x}^{(t_i)}=(1,2,\cdots, N_{t_i})$\end{document}. Here we want to get the emission probabilities for each measurement ω^(ti)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{(t_i)}$\end{document}, which can be used by the outer HMM. Detailed steps are provided in the following Appendix 2–algorithm 3.

tr.th: Appendix 2–algorithm 3. Emission probabilities of linear model.

tr.td: Input: y(ti)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol y^{(t_{i})}$\end{document}, x(ti)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol x^{(t_{i})}$\end{document}Output: ω^(ti)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol \omega }^{(t_{i})}$\end{document}, β0(ti)\begin{document}$\beta_{0}^{(t_{i})}$\end{document}, β1(ti)\begin{document}$\beta_{1}^{(t_{i})}$\end{document},σϵ(ti)\begin{document}$\sigma_{\epsilon } ^{(t_{i})}$\end{document}initialize ω^(ti)\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol \omega }^{(t_{i})}$\end{document} to a 1 × Nti all zero vector.Fit a linear model of y(ti) versus x(ti), obtain the maximum likelihood estimates β0(ti),β1(ti)\begin{document}$\beta ^{(t_{i})}_{0},\beta ^{(t_{i})}_{1}$\end{document} and σϵ(ti)\begin{document}$\sigma ^{(t_{i})}_{\epsilon }$\end{document}Calculate the maximum log joint likelihood log⁡p(y(ti)|x(ti),β0(ti),β1(ti),σϵ(ti))\begin{document}$\log p(\boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)} | \boldsymbol{x}^{(t_i)}, \beta_0^{(t_i)}, \beta_1^{(t_i)}, \sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_i)})$\end{document} = log⁡N(y(ti)−β1(ti)x(ti)−β0(ti)∣0,(σϵ(ti))2)\begin{document}$\log N(\boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)}- \beta_{1} ^{(t_i)} \boldsymbol{x}^{(t_{i})}-\beta_0^{(t_i)}\mid0, (\sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_i)})^{2})$\end{document}Set each element of ω^ti\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol \omega }^{t_{i}}$\end{document} to exp (1Ntilog⁡p(y(ti)∣x(ti),β0(ti),β1(ti),σϵ(ti)))\begin{document}$\left( \frac{1}{N_{t_i}} \log p\!\left( \boldsymbol{y}^{(t_i)} \mid \boldsymbol{x}^{(t_i)}, \beta_0^{(t_i)}, \beta_1^{(t_i)}, \sigma_{\epsilon}^{(t_i)} \right) \right)$\end{document}

sec.title: Emission probabilities of inner HMM
sec.p: This section describes how to obtain the approximated emission probabilities of the outer HMM for a the ith base block y(bi)=(y1(bi),y2(bi),⋯,yNbi(bi))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}=(y_{1}^{(b_i)}, y_{2}^{(b_i)},\cdots, y_{N_{b_i}}^{(b_i)})$\end{document}, which is modeled using an inner HMM. Here bi=2i−1\begin{document}$b_{i}=2i-1$\end{document} and i∈{1,2,⋯,K}\begin{document}$i\in\{1,2,\cdots,K\}$\end{document}.
For the inner HMM, we list all the parameters in Appendix 2—table 3.

caption.title: Parameters of the inner HMM for the bi\begin{document}$b_{i}$\end{document} th block.

tr.td: Transition probabilities between hidden states(‘curr’, ‘prev’, ‘next’, ‘noise’), specified in Appendix 2—table 2
probability of the first hidden state, πkinner=0.25\begin{document}$\pi^{inner}_{k}=0.25$\end{document} , wherek∈{1,2,3,4}\begin{document}$k\in\{1,2,3,4\}$\end{document}
Gaussian std ofi th base block
Gaussian mean of i−1\begin{document}$i-1$\end{document} th base block
Gaussian std of i−1\begin{document}$i-1$\end{document} th base block
Gaussian mean of i+1\begin{document}$i+1$\end{document} th base block
Gaussian std of i+1\begin{document}$i+1$\end{document} th base block

sec.p: We want to obtain the approximated emission probabilities ω^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{(b_i)}$\end{document}, hidden states h^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{h}}^{(b_i)}$\end{document}, estimated parameters ϕ^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}^{(b_i)}$\end{document} and σ^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}^{(b_i)}$\end{document} for the ith base block y(bi)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(b_i)}$\end{document}. To achieve this, we follow the Forward-backward algorithm in Bishop and Nasrabadi, 2006 (Chapter 13.2). The general idea of the Forward-backward algorithm is to use a Q\begin{document}$Q$\end{document}-function to approximate the marginal likelihood, then use an expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm for the parameter estimation.

We introduce the following notations for the Forward-backward algorithm. We use Z=(z1,z2,⋯,zNbi)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{Z}= (\boldsymbol{z}_{1}, \boldsymbol{z}_{2}, \cdots, \boldsymbol {z}_{N_{b_i}})$\end{document} to denote the hidden states, where Z\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{Z}$\end{document} is a Nbi×4\begin{document}$N_{b_i}\times 4$\end{document} matrix, zj=(zj1,zj2,zj3,zj4)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{z}_{j}=(z_{j1},z_{j2}, z_{j3},z_{j4})$\end{document} and zjk∈{0,1}\begin{document}$z_{jk}\in\{0,1\}$\end{document}. If zjk\begin{document}$z_{jk}$\end{document} is 1, then it means hj\begin{document}$h_{j}$\end{document} corresponds to the kth hidden state. We use α\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\alpha}$\end{document} (Nbi×4\begin{document}$N_{b_i}\times 4$\end{document}) to denote the forward probabilities and β\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\beta}$\end{document} (Nbi×4\begin{document}$N_{b_i}\times 4$\end{document}) to denote the backward probabilities. γ\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\gamma}$\end{document} (Nbi×4\begin{document}$N_{b_i}\times 4$\end{document}) denotes the posterior distribution calculated from α\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\alpha}$\end{document} and β\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\beta}$\end{document}. ξ\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\xi}$\end{document} ((Nbi−1)×4×4\begin{document}$(N_{b_i}-1)\times 4 \times 4$\end{document}) denotes the joint distribution of two successive hidden states. We use θ=(ϕ(bi),σ(bi))\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\theta}=(\phi^{(b_i)}, \sigma^{(b_i)})$\end{document} to denote all parameters to be estimated.(1)Q(θ,θold)=∑Zp(Z∣y(bi),θold)log⁡p(y(bi),Z∣θ)=∑k=14γ1klog⁡πkinner+∑n=2Nbi∑j=14∑k=14ξn−1,j,klog⁡Tjkinner+∑n=1Nbi{γn1log⁡p(yn(bi)∣ϕ(bi),σ(bi))+γn2log⁡p(yn(bi)∣ϕ(bi−1),σ(bi−1))+γn3log⁡p(yn(bi)∣ϕ(bi+1),σ(bi+1))+γn4log⁡p(yn(bi)∣lb,ub)}\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll} Q\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}\right)&= \sum_{\boldsymbol{Z}} p\left(\boldsymbol{Z} \mid \boldsymbol{y}^{\left(b_i\right)}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}\right) \log p\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{\left(b_i\right)}, \boldsymbol{Z} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}\right) \\ & = \sum_{k=1}^4 \gamma_{1 k} \log \pi_k^{inner }+\sum_{n=2}^{N_{b_i}} \sum_{j=1}^4 \sum_{k=1}^4 \xi_{n-1, j, k} \log T_{j k}^{inner } \\ & + \sum_{n=1}^{N_{b_i}}\left\{\gamma_{n 1} \log p\left(y_n^{\left(b_i\right)} \mid \phi^{\left(b_i\right)}, \sigma^{\left(b_i\right)}\right)+\gamma_{n 2} \log p\left(y_n^{\left(b_i\right)} \mid \phi^{\left(b_{i-1}\right)}, \sigma^{\left(b_{i-1}\right)}\right)\right. \\ & \left.\quad+\gamma_{n 3} \log p\left(y_n^{\left(b_i\right)} \mid \phi^{\left(b_{i+1}\right)}, \sigma^{\left(b_{i+1}\right)}\right)+\gamma_{n 4} \log p\left(y_n^{\left(b_i\right)} \mid l b, u b\right)\right\} \end{array}$$\end{document}

Here we only need to update α\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\alpha}$\end{document}, β\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\beta}$\end{document}, γ\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\gamma}$\end{document}, ξ\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{\xi}$\end{document}, ϕ(bi)\begin{document}$\phi^{(b_i)}$\end{document} and σ(bi)\begin{document}$\sigma^{(b_i)}$\end{document} in the inference process of inner HMM for ith base block. Our inference algorithm is a modification of the standard Forward-backward algorithm based on the defined Q\begin{document}$Q$\end{document}-function, as described in Appendix 2–algorithm 4.

tr.th: Appendix 2–algorithm 4. Inference algorithm of inner HMM.

tr.td: Input: y(bi),ϕ(bi),σ(bi),ϕ(bi−1),σ(bi−1),ϕ(bi+1),σ(bi+1),Tinner,πinner,lb,ub\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(bi)},\phi ^{(bi)}, \sigma ^{(bi)}, \phi ^{(bi-1)},\sigma ^{(bi-1)},\phi ^{(bi+1)},\sigma ^{(bi+1)},T^{inner},\boldsymbol \pi ^{inner},lb,ub$\end{document}Output: ω^(bi),h^(bi),ϕ^(bi),σ^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol \omega}^{(bi)},\hat {\boldsymbol h}^{(bi)},\hat {\phi }^{(bi)},\hat {\sigma }^{(bi)}$\end{document}Initialize h^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol h}^{(bi)}$\end{document} and ω^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol \omega}^{(bi)}$\end{document} to the 1×Nbi\begin{document}$1\times N_{b_{i}}$\end{document} all zero vector.Initialize α, β, γ, ξ // EM algorithmfor round = 1, 2, · · · , R do E-step: update α, β, γ, and ξ given y(bi),ϕ(bi),σ(bi),ϕ(bi−1),σ(bi−1),ϕ(bi+1),σ(bi+1),Tinner,πinner,lb,ub\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(bi)},\phi ^{(bi)}, \sigma ^{(bi)}, \phi ^{(bi-1)},\sigma ^{(bi-1)},\phi ^{(bi+1)},\sigma ^{(bi+1)},T^{inner},\boldsymbol{\pi} ^{inner},lb,ub$\end{document} M-step: calculate ϕ^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat {\phi }^{(bi)}$\end{document} and σ^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat {\sigma }^{(bi)}$\end{document} by Equation 2 and Equation 3 ϕ(bi)←ϕ^(bi)\begin{document}$\phi ^{(bi)}\leftarrow \hat {\phi }^{(bi)}$\end{document} σ(bi)←σ^(bi)\begin{document}$\sigma ^{(bi)}\leftarrow \hat {\sigma }^{(bi)}$\end{document} Calculate the Q-function (log marginal likelihood) q^\begin{document}$\hat {q}$\end{document} given all estimated parametersendDerive h^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol h}^{(b_{i})}$\end{document} from γ\begin{document}$\gamma $\end{document} by taking the state with highest probability for each data point, i.e. hn(bi)=argmax([γn1,γn2,γn3,γn4])\begin{document}$h^{(b_{i})}_{n}=argmax\,([\gamma _{n1},\gamma _{n2},\gamma _{n3},\gamma _{n4}])$\end{document}Set each element of ω^(bi)\begin{document}$\hat {\boldsymbol \omega }^{(b_{i})}$\end{document} to exp(q^/Nbi)\begin{document}${\rm exp}(\hat q/N_{b_{i}})$\end{document}.

sec.title: GPU-accelerated parameter inference
sec.p: Given the general inference algorithm (‘General inference algorithm’) and algorithms for calculating the emission probabilities for the inner HMM (Appendix 2–algorithm 4) and linear model (Appendix 2–algorithm 3), we have the full algorithm for the parameter inference. The actual inference, however, can be really slow since the input fast5 data are huge. Here we use heuristics and GPU parallelization to accelerate the parameter inference.

We divide the raw current signal of each read y\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}$\end{document} into overlapping regions, which start at an atomic base block and also end with an atomic base block. As shown in Appendix 2—figure 2, we process the rth region y(r)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{y}^{(r)}$\end{document} independently to get the segmentation results z(r)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{z}^{(r)}$\end{document}. This process is based on the following heuristics: the segmentation result of one region will not affect another region when they are far away from each other. The inference is performed in two rounds. In the first round, we obtain the segmentation results for non-overlapping regions. The center part of the segmentation is not affected by the flank parts, which may be affected by neighboring regions. In the second round, we merge relevant parts of neighboring regions near the touch point as a new region based on segmentation results of the first round. After that, we perform the inference for the new regions to get the final segmentations for the whole read.

We use GPU to parallelize the inference algorithm of inner HMM (Alg. 4). For each region, we first enumerate all possible paths and calculate the likelihood for each candidate path (‘Enumeration of hidden states of outer HMM’). As shown in Appendix 2—figure 2, we need to perform inner HMM parameter inference for different paths of different regions of one read. We consider parallelizing the computation of different merged blocks seg(i,j)\begin{document}$seg(i,j)$\end{document} in different paths of different regions. We first collect all merged blocks and sort them by size and only keep unique blocks, which can be identified by the combinatorial key of region index, start atomic block index, end atomic block index. Then we run the inner HMM inference (Alg. 4) of each merged block on a GPU core. Note that all GPU cores perform the same computation step of Alg. 4 simultaneously, but for different merged blocks.

caption.title: Illustration of enumeration of hidden states of outer HMM.
caption.p: (a) Raw signal and initialized to atomic segmentations. (b) Examples of the full configuration set G=(g1,g2,⋯,gi,⋯,gM)\begin{document}$\mathcal{G}= (\boldsymbol{g}_{1}, \boldsymbol{g}_{2}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{g}_{i}, \cdots, \boldsymbol{g}_{M})$\end{document}.

caption.title: Illustration of GPU-accelerated parameter inference.
